sourceMovie.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Star Wars: The Last Jedi works as a adventure movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The adventure and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Guardians of the Galaxy (2014) belongs to the same conversation as Star Wars: The Last Jedi — both anchor their adventure in action emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Star Wars
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Star Wars: The Last Jedi's adventure stayed with you, Star Wars (1977) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The action through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: The Force Awakens
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Star Wars: The Last Jedi excels in adventure, Star Wars: The Force Awakens (2015) doubles down on action to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: The Empire Strikes Back
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Empire Strikes Back (1980) is the natural next step after Star Wars: The Last Jedi: same adventure DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and action elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Return of the Jedi
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Star Wars: The Last Jedi who respond to its adventure will find Return of the Jedi (1983) immediately familiar. Both films share a action sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Star Wars: The Last Jedi fans keep landing on Rogue One: A Star Wars Story (2016) — the adventure is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace draws from the same adventure well as Star Wars: The Last Jedi, channeling action in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1999 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith (2005) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Star Wars: The Last Jedi later expanded. If Star Wars: The Last Jedi resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: Episode II - Attack of the Clones
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure appeal of Star Wars: The Last Jedi — that tense edge, the action — translates cleanly into Star Wars: Episode II - Attack of the Clones (2002). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker (2019) clearly understood what makes adventure work — the same quality that keeps Star Wars: The Last Jedi rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Star Wars: The Last Jedi?
faq.answer: Try Guardians of the Galaxy, Star Wars, Star Wars: The Force Awakens—each shares DNA with Star Wars: The Last Jedi while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Star Wars: The Last Jedi?
faq.answer: Star Wars: The Last Jedi (2017) is best described as Adventure, Action, Science Fiction. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Star Wars: The Last Jedi part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Star Wars Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
